When a datetime is\n   123\t        read from the database, it is always returned in this time zone.\n   124\t\n   125\t        When the database backend supports time zones, it doesn't matter which\n   126\t        time zone Django uses, as long as aware datetimes are used everywhere.\n   127\t        Other users connecting to the database can choose their own time zone.\n   128\t\n   129\t        When the database backend doesn't support time zones, the time zone\n   130\t        Django uses may be constrained by the requirements of other users of\n   131\t        the database.\n   132\t        \"\"\"\n   133\t        if not settings.USE_TZ:\n   134\t            return None\n   135\t        elif self.settings_dict['TIME_ZONE'] is None:\n   136\t            return timezone.utc\n   137\t        else:\n   138\t            return pytz.timezone(self.settings_dict['TIME_ZONE'])\n   139\t\n   140\t    @cached_property\n   141\t    def timezone_name(self):\n   142\t        \"\"\"\n   143\t        Name of the time zone of the database connection.\n   144\t        \"\"\"\n   145\t        if not settings.USE_TZ:\n   146\t            return settings.TIME_ZONE\n   147\t        elif self.settings_dict['TIME_ZONE'] is None:\n   148\t            return 'UTC'\n   149\t        else:\n   150\t            return self.settings_dict['TIME_ZONE']\n   151\t\n   152\t    @property\n   153\t    def queries_logged(self):\n   154\t        return self.force_debug_cursor or settings.DEBUG\n   155\t\n   156\t    @property\n   157\t    def queries(self):\n   158\t        if len(self.queries_log) == self.queries_log.maxlen:\n   159\t            warnings.warn(\n   160\t                \"Limit for query logging exceeded, only the last {} queries \"\n   161\t                \"will be returned.\".format(self.queries_log.maxlen))\n   162\t        return list(self.queries_log)\n   163\t\n   164\t    # Assume that the connection is closed.\"\"\"\n   187\t        # Check for invalid configurations.\n   188\t        self.check_settings()\n   189\t        # In case the previous connection was closed while in an atomic block\n   190\t        self.in_atomic_block = False\n   191\t        self.savepoint_ids = []\n   192\t        self.needs_rollback = False\n   193\t        # ##### Generic wrappers for PEP-249 connection methods #####\n   255\t\n   256\t    @async_unsafe\n   257\t    def cursor(self):\n   258\t        \"\"\"Create a cursor, opening a connection if necessary.\"\"\"\n   259\t        return self._cursor()\n   260\t\n   261\t    @async_unsafe\n   262\t    def commit(self):\n   263\t        \"\"\"Commit a transaction and reset the dirty flag.\"\"\"\n   264\t        self.validate_thread_sharing()\n   265\t        self.validate_no_atomic_block()\n   266\t        self._commit()\n   267\t        # {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:9","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"     1\t==========\n     2\tTime zones\n     3\t==========\n     4\t\n     5\t.. _time-zones-overview:\n     6\t\n     7\tOverview\n     8\t========\n     9\t\n    10\tWhen support for time zones is enabled, Django stores datetime information in\n    11\tUTC in the database, uses time-zone-aware datetime objects internally, and\n    12\ttranslates them to the end user's time zone in templates and forms.\n    13\t\n    14\tThis is handy if your users live in more than one time zone and you want to\n    15\tdisplay datetime information according to each user's wall clock.\n    16\t\n    17\tEven if your website is available in only one time zone, it's still good\n    18\tpractice to store data in UTC in your database. The main reason is daylight\n    19\tsaving time (DST). Many countries have a system of DST, where clocks are moved\n    20\tforward in spring and backward in autumn. If you're working in local time,\n    21\tyou're likely to encounter errors twice a year, when the transitions happen.\n    22\t(The pytz_ documentation discusses `these issues`_ in greater detail.) This\n    23\tprobably doesn't matter for your blog, but it's a problem if you over-bill or\n    24\tunder-bill your customers by one hour, twice a year, every year. The solution\n    25\tto this problem is to use UTC in the code and use local time only when\n    26\tinteracting with end users.\n    27\t\n    28\tTime zone support is disabled by default. To enable it, set :setting:`USE_TZ =\n    29\tTrue <USE_TZ>` in your settings file.\n    30\t\n    31\t.. note::\n    32\t\n    33\t    In Django 5.0, time zone support will be enabled by default.\n    34\t\n    35\tBy default, time zone support uses pytz_, which is installed when you install\n    36\tDjango; Django also supports the use of other time zone implementations like\n    37\t:mod:`zoneinfo` by passing :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` objects directly to\n    38\tfunctions in :mod:`django.utils.timezone`.\n    39\t\n    40\t.. versionchanged:: 3.2\n    41\t\n    42\t    Support for non-``pytz`` timezone implementations was added.\n    43\t\n    44\t.. note::\n    45\t\n    46\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n    47\t    startproject <startproject>` includes :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>`\n    48\t    for convenience.\n    49\t\n    50\tIf you're wrestling with a particular problem, start with the :ref:`time zone\n    51\tFAQ <time-zones-faq>`.\n    52\t\n    53\tConcepts\n    54\t========\n    55\t\n    56\t.. _naive_vs_aware_datetimes:\n    57\t\n    58\tNaive and aware datetime objects\n    59\t--------------------------------\n    60\t\n    61\tPython's :class:`datetime.datetime` objects have a ``tzinfo`` attribute that\n    62\tcan be used to store time zone information, represented as an instance of a\n    63\tsubclass of :class:`datetime.tzinfo`. When this attribute is set and describes\n    64\tan offset, a datetime object is **aware**. Otherwise, it's **naive**.\n    65\t\n    66\tYou can use :func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware` and\n    67\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive` to determine whether datetimes are\n    68\taware or naive.\n    69\t\n    70\tWhen time zone support is disabled, Django uses naive datetime objects in local\n    71\ttime. This is sufficient for many use cases. In this mode, to obtain the\n    72\tcurrent time, you would write::\n    73\t\n    74\t    import datetime\n    75\t\n    76\t    now = datetime.datetime.now()\n    77\t\n    78\tWhen time zone support is enabled (:setting:`USE_TZ=True <USE_TZ>`), Django uses\n    79\ttime-zone-aware datetime objects. If your code creates datetime objects, they\n    80\tshould be aware too. In this mode, the example above becomes::\n    81\t\n    82\t    from django.utils import timezone\n    83\t\n    84\t    now = timezone.now()\n    85\t\n    86\t.. warning::\n    87\t\n    88\t    Dealing with aware datetime objects isn't always intuitive. For instance,\n    89\t    the ``tzinfo`` argument of the standard datetime constructor doesn't work\n    90\t    reliably for time zones with DST. Using UTC is generally safe; if you're\n    91\t    using other time zones, you should review the `pytz`_ documentation\n    92\t    carefully.\n    93\t\n    94\t.. note::\n    95\t\n    96\t    Python's :class:`datetime.time` objects also feature a ``tzinfo``\n    97\t    attribute, and PostgreSQL has a matching ``time with time zone`` type.\n    98\t    However, as PostgreSQL's docs put it, this type \"exhibits properties which\n    99\t    lead to questionable usefulness\".\n   100\t\n   101\t    Django only supports naive time objects and will raise an exception if you\n   102\t    attempt to save an aware time object, as a timezone for a time with no\n   103\t    associated date does not make sense.\n   104\t\n   105\t.. _naive-datetime-objects:\n   106\t\n   107\tInterpretation of naive datetime objects\n   108\t----------------------------------------\n   109\t\n   110\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, Django still accepts naive datetime\n   111\tobjects, in order to preserve backwards-compatibility. When the database layer\n   112\treceives one, it attempts to make it aware by interpreting it in the\n   113\t:ref:`default time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and raises a warning.\n   114\t\n   115\tUnfortunately, during DST transitions, some datetimes don't exist or are\n   116\tambiguous. In such situations, pytz_ raises an exception. That's why you should\n   117\talways create aware datetime objects when time zone support is enabled.\n   118\t\n   119\tIn practice, this is rarely an issue. Django gives you aware datetime objects\n   120\tin the models and forms, and most often, new datetime objects are created from\n   121\texisting ones through :class:`~datetime.timedelta` arithmetic. The only\n   122\tdatetime that's often created in application code is the current time, and\n   123\t:func:`timezone.now() <django.utils.timezone.now>` automatically does the\n   124\tright thing.\n   125\t\n   126\t.. _default-current-time-zone:\n   127\t\n   128\tDefault time zone and current time zone\n   129\t---------------------------------------\n   130\t\n   131\tThe **default time zone** is the time zone defined by the :setting:`TIME_ZONE`\n   132\tsetting.\n   133\t\n   134\tThe **current time zone** is the time zone that's used for rendering.\n   135\t\n   136\tYou should set the current time zone to the end user's actual time zone with\n   137\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.activate`. Otherwise, the default time zone is\n   138\tused.\n   139\t\n   140\t.. note::\n   141\t\n   142\t    As explained in the documentation of :setting:`TIME_ZONE`, Django sets\n   143\t    environment variables so that its process runs in the default time zone.\n   144\t    This happens regardless of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ` and of the\n   145\t    current time zone.\n   146\t\n   147\t    When :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this is useful to preserve\n   148\t    backwards-compatibility with applications that still rely on local time.\n   149\t    However, :ref:`as explained above <naive-datetime-objects>`, this isn't\n   150\t    entirely reliable, and you should always work with aware datetimes in UTC\n   151\t    in your own code. For instance, use :meth:`~datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp`\n   152\t    and set the ``tz`` parameter to :data:`~django.utils.timezone.utc`.\n   153\t\n   154\tSelecting the current time zone\n   155\t-------------------------------\n   156\t\n   157\tThe current time zone is the equivalent of the current :term:`locale <locale\n   158\tname>` for translations. However, there's no equivalent of the\n   159\t``Accept-Language`` HTTP header that Django could use to determine the user's\n   160\ttime zone automatically. Instead, Django provides :ref:`time zone selection\n   161\tfunctions <time-zone-selection-functions>`. Use them to build the time zone\n   162\tselection logic that makes sense for you.\n   163\t\n   164\tMost websites that care about time zones ask users in which time zone they live\n   165\tand store this information in the user's profile. For anonymous users, they use\n   166\tthe time zone of their primary audience or UTC. pytz_ provides helpers_, like a\n   167\tlist of time zones per country, that you can use to pre-select the most likely\n   168\tchoices.\n   169\t\n   170\tHere's an example that stores the current timezone in the session. (It skips\n   171\terror handling entirely for the sake of simplicity.)\n   172\t\n   173\tAdd the following middleware to :setting:`MIDDLEWARE`::\n   174\t\n   175\t    import pytz\n   176\t\n   177\t    from django.utils import timezone\n   178\t\n   179\t    class TimezoneMiddleware:\n   180\t        def __init__(self, get_response):\n   181\t            self.get_response = get_response\n   182\t\n   183\t        def __call__(self, request):\n   184\t            tzname = request.session.get('django_timezone')\n   185\t            if tzname:\n   186\t                timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(tzname))\n   187\t            else:\n   188\t                timezone.deactivate()\n   189\t            return self.get_response(request)\n   190\t\n   191\tCreate a view that can set the current timezone::\n   192\t\n   193\t    from django.shortcuts import redirect, render\n   194\t\n   195\t    def set_timezone(request):\n   196\t        if request.method == 'POST':\n   197\t            request.session['django_timezone'] = request.POST['timezone']\n   198\t            return redirect('/')\n   199\t        else:\n   200\t            return render(request, 'template.html', {'timezones': pytz.common_timezones})\n   201\t\n   202\tInclude a form in ``template.html`` that will ``POST`` to this view:\n   203\t\n   204\t.. code-block:: html+django\n   205\t\n   206\t    {% load tz %}\n   207\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   208\t    <form action=\"{% url 'set_timezone' %}\" method=\"POST\">\n   209\t        {% csrf_token %}\n   210\t        <label for=\"timezone\">Time zone:</label>\n   211\t        <select name=\"timezone\">\n   212\t            {% for tz in timezones %}\n   213\t            <option value=\"{{ tz }}\"{% if tz == TIME_ZONE %} selected{% endif %}>{{ tz }}</option>\n   214\t            {% endfor %}\n   215\t        </select>\n   216\t        <input type=\"submit\" value=\"Set\">\n   217\t    </form>\n   218\t\n   219\t.. _time-zones-in-forms:\n   220\t\n   221\tTime zone aware input in forms\n   222\t==============================\n   223\t\n   224\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django interprets datetimes entered in\n   225\tforms in the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and returns\n   226\taware datetime objects in ``cleaned_data``.\n   227\t\n   228\tIf the current time zone raises an exception for datetimes that don't exist or\n   229\tare ambiguous because they fall in a DST transition (the timezones provided by\n   230\tpytz_ do this), such datetimes will be reported as invalid values.\n   231\t\n   232\t.. _time-zones-in-templates:\n   233\t\n   234\tTime zone aware output in templates\n   235\t===================================\n   236\t\n   237\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django converts aware datetime objects to\n   238\tthe :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` when they're rendered\n   239\tin templates. This behaves very much like :doc:`format localization\n   240\t</topics/i18n/formatting>`.\n   241\t\n   242\t.. warning::\n   243\t\n   244\t    Django doesn't convert naive datetime objects, because they could be\n   245\t    ambiguous, and because your code should never produce naive datetimes when\n   246\t    time zone support is enabled. However, you can force conversion with the\n   247\t    template filters described below.\n   248\t\n   249\tConversion to local time isn't always appropriate -- you may be generating\n   250\toutput for computers rather than for humans. The following filters and tags,\n   251\tprovided by the ``tz`` template tag library, allow you to control the time zone\n   252\tconversions.\n   253\t\n   254\t.. highlight:: html+django\n   255\t\n   256\tTemplate tags\n   257\t-------------\n   258\t\n   259\t.. templatetag:: localtime\n   260\t\n   261\t``localtime``\n   262\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   263\t\n   264\tEnables or disables conversion of aware datetime objects to the current time\n   265\tzone in the contained block.\n   266\t\n   267\tThis tag has exactly the same effects as the :setting:`USE_TZ` setting as far\n   268\tas the template engine is concerned. It allows a more fine grained control of\n   269\tconversion.\n   270\t\n   271\tTo activate or deactivate conversion for a template block, use::\n   272\t\n   273\t    {% load tz %}\n   274\t\n   275\t    {% localtime on %}\n   276\t        {{ value }}\n   277\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   278\t\n   279\t    {% localtime off %}\n   280\t        {{ value }}\n   281\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   282\t\n   283\t.. note::\n   284\t\n   285\t    The value of :setting:`USE_TZ` isn't respected inside of a\n   286\t    ``{% localtime %}`` block.\n   287\t\n   288\t.. templatetag:: timezone\n   289\t\n   290\t``timezone``\n   291\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   292\t\n   293\tSets or unsets the current time zone in the contained block. When the current\n   294\ttime zone is unset, the default time zone applies.\n   295\t\n   296\t::\n   297\t\n   298\t    {% load tz %}\n   299\t\n   300\t    {% timezone \"Europe/Paris\" %}\n   301\t        Paris time: {{ value }}\n   302\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   303\t\n   304\t    {% timezone None %}\n   305\t        Server time: {{ value }}\n   306\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   307\t\n   308\t.. templatetag:: get_current_timezone\n   309\t\n   310\t``get_current_timezone``\n   311\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   312\t\n   313\tYou can get the name of the current time zone using the\n   314\t``get_current_timezone`` tag::\n   315\t\n   316\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   317\t\n   318\tAlternatively, you can activate the\n   319\t:func:`~django.template.context_processors.tz` context processor and\n   320\tuse the ``TIME_ZONE`` context variable.\n   321\t\n   322\tTemplate filters\n   323\t----------------\n   324\t\n   325\tThese filters accept both aware and naive datetimes. For conversion purposes,\n   326\tthey assume that naive datetimes are in the default time zone. They always\n   327\treturn aware datetimes.\n   328\t\n   329\t.. templatefilter:: localtime\n   330\t\n   331\t``localtime``\n   332\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   333\t\n   334\tForces conversion of a single value to the current time zone.\n   335\t\n   336\tFor example::\n   337\t\n   338\t    {% load tz %}\n   339\t\n   340\t    {{ value|localtime }}\n   341\t\n   342\t.. templatefilter:: utc\n   343\t\n   344\t``utc``\n   345\t~~~~~~~\n   346\t\n   347\tForces conversion of a single value to UTC.\n   348\t\n   349\tFor example::\n   350\t\n   351\t    {% load tz %}\n   352\t\n   353\t    {{ value|utc }}\n   354\t\n   355\t.. templatefilter:: timezone\n   356\t\n   357\t``timezone``\n   358\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   359\t\n   360\tForces conversion of a single value to an arbitrary timezone.\n   361\t\n   362\tThe argument must be an instance of a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` subclass or a\n   363\ttime zone name.\n   364\t\n   365\tFor example::\n   366\t\n   367\t    {% load tz %}\n   368\t\n   369\t    {{ value|timezone:\"Europe/Paris\" }}\n   370\t\n   371\t.. highlight:: python\n   372\t\n   373\t.. _time-zones-migration-guide:\n   374\t\n   375\tMigration guide\n   376\t===============\n   377\t\n   378\tHere's how to migrate a project that was started before Django supported time\n   379\tzones.\n   380\t\n   381\tDatabase\n   382\t--------\n   383\t\n   384\tPostgreSQL\n   385\t~~~~~~~~~~\n   386\t\n   387\tThe PostgreSQL backend stores datetimes as ``timestamp with time zone``. In\n   388\tpractice, this means it converts datetimes from the connection's time zone to\n   389\tUTC on storage, and from UTC to the connection's time zone on retrieval.\n   390\t\n   391\tAs a consequence, if you're using PostgreSQL, you can switch between ``USE_TZ\n   392\t= False`` and ``USE_TZ = True`` freely. The database connection's time zone\n   393\twill be set to :setting:`TIME_ZONE` or ``UTC`` respectively, so that Django\n   394\tobtains correct datetimes in all cases. You don't need to perform any data\n   395\tconversions.\n   396\t\n   397\tOther databases\n   398\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   399\t\n   400\tOther backends store datetimes without time zone information. If you switch\n   401\tfrom ``USE_TZ = False`` to ``USE_TZ = True``, you must convert your data from\n   402\tlocal time to UTC -- which isn't deterministic if your local time has DST.\n   403\t\n   404\tCode\n   405\t----\n   406\t\n   407\tThe first step is to add :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>` to your settings\n   408\tfile. At this point, things should mostly work. If you create naive datetime\n   409\tobjects in your code, Django makes them aware when necessary.\n   410\t\n   411\tHowever, these conversions may fail around DST transitions, which means you\n   412\taren't getting the full benefits of time zone support yet. Also, you're likely\n   413\tto run into a few problems because it's impossible to compare a naive datetime\n   414\twith an aware datetime. Since Django now gives you aware datetimes, you'll get\n   415\texceptions wherever you compare a datetime that comes from a model or a form\n   416\twith a naive datetime that you've created in your code.\n   417\t\n   418\tSo the second step is to refactor your code wherever you instantiate datetime\n   419\tobjects to make them aware. This can be done incrementally.\n   420\t:mod:`django.utils.timezone` defines some handy helpers for compatibility\n   421\tcode: :func:`~django.utils.timezone.now`,\n   422\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware`,\n   423\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive`,\n   424\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_aware`, and\n   425\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_naive`.\n   426\t\n   427\tFinally, in order to help you locate code that needs upgrading, Django raises\n   428\ta warning when you attempt to save a naive datetime to the database::\n   429\t\n   430\t    RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField ModelName.field_name received a naive\n   431\t    datetime (2012-01-01 00:00:00) while time zone support is active.\n   432\t\n   433\tDuring development, you can turn such warnings into exceptions and get a\n   434\ttraceback by adding the following to your settings file::\n   435\t\n   436\t    import warnings\n   437\t    warnings.filterwarnings(\n   438\t        'error', r\"DateTimeField .* received a naive datetime\",\n   439\t        RuntimeWarning, r'django\\.db\\.models\\.fields',\n   440\t    )\n   441\t\n   442\tFixtures\n   443\t--------\n   444\t\n   445\tWhen serializing an aware datetime, the UTC offset is included, like this::\n   446\t\n   447\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30+03:00\"\n   448\t\n   449\tWhile for a naive datetime, it isn't::\n   450\t\n   451\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\"\n   452\t\n   453\tFor models with :class:`~django.db.models.DateTimeField`\\ s, this difference\n   454\tmakes it impossible to write a fixture that works both with and without time\n   455\tzone support.\n   456\t\n   457\tFixtures generated with ``USE_TZ = False``, or before Django 1.4, use the\n   458\t\"naive\" format. If your project contains such fixtures, after you enable time\n   459\tzone support, you'll see :exc:`RuntimeWarning`\\ s when you load them. To get\n   460\trid of the warnings, you must convert your fixtures to the \"aware\" format.\n   461\t\n   462\tYou can regenerate fixtures with :djadmin:`loaddata` then :djadmin:`dumpdata`.\n   463\tOr, if they're small enough, you can edit them to add the UTC offset that\n   464\tmatches your :setting:`TIME_ZONE` to each serialized datetime.\n   465\t\n   466\t.. _time-zones-faq:\n   467\t\n   468\tFAQ\n   469\t===\n   470\t\n   471\tSetup\n   472\t-----\n   473\t\n   474\t#. **I don't need multiple time zones. Should I enable time zone support?**\n   475\t\n   476\t   Yes. When time zone support is enabled, Django uses a more accurate model\n   477\t   of local time. This shields you from subtle and unreproducible bugs around\n   478\t   daylight saving time (DST) transitions.\n   479\t\n   480\t   When you enable time zone support, you'll encounter some errors because\n   481\t   you're using naive datetimes where Django expects aware datetimes. Such\n   482\t   errors show up when running tests. You'll quickly learn how to avoid invalid\n   483\t   operations.\n   484\t\n   485\t   On the other hand, bugs caused by the lack of time zone support are much\n   486\t   harder to prevent, diagnose and fix. Anything that involves scheduled tasks\n   487\t   or datetime arithmetic is a candidate for subtle bugs that will bite you\n   488\t   only once or twice a year.\n   489\t\n   490\t   For these reasons, time zone support is enabled by default in new projects,\n   491\t   and you should keep it unless you have a very good reason not to.\n   492\t\n   493\t#. **I've enabled time zone support. Am I safe?**\n   494\t\n   495\t   Maybe. You're better protected from DST-related bugs, but you can still\n   496\t   shoot yourself in the foot by carelessly turning naive datetimes into aware\n   497\t   datetimes, and vice-versa.\n   498\t\n   499\t   If your application connects to other systems -- for instance, if it queries\n   500\t   a web service -- make sure datetimes are properly specified. To transmit\n   501\t   datetimes safely, their representation should include the UTC offset, or\n   502\t   their values should be in UTC (or both!).\n   503\t\n   504\t   Finally, our calendar system contains interesting edge cases. For example,\n   505\t   you can't always subtract one year directly from a given date::\n   506\t\n   507\t       >>> import datetime\n   508\t       >>> def one_year_before(value):  # Wrong example.\n   509\t       ...     return value.replace(year=value.year - 1)\n   510\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 1, 10, 0))\n   511\t       datetime.datetime(2011, 3, 1, 10, 0)\n   512\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 29, 10, 0))\n   513\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   514\t       ...\n   515\t       ValueError: day is out of range for month\n   516\t\n   517\t   To implement such a function correctly, you must decide whether 2012-02-29\n   518\t   minus one year is 2011-02-28 or 2011-03-01, which depends on your business\n   519\t   requirements.\n   520\t\n   521\t#. **How do I interact with a database that stores datetimes in local time?**\n   522\t\n   523\t   Set the :setting:`TIME_ZONE <DATABASE-TIME_ZONE>` option to the appropriate\n   524\t   time zone for this database in the :setting:`DATABASES` setting.\n   525\t\n   526\t   This is useful for connecting to a database that doesn't support time zones\n   527\t   and that isn't managed by Django when :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``.\n   528\t\n   529\tTroubleshooting\n   530\t---------------\n   531\t\n   532\t#. **My application crashes with** ``TypeError: can't compare offset-naive``\n   533\t   ``and offset-aware datetimes`` **-- what's wrong?**\n   534\t\n   535\t   Let's reproduce this error by comparing a naive and an aware datetime::\n   536\t\n   537\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   538\t       >>> aware = timezone.now()\n   539\t       >>> naive = timezone.make_naive(aware)\n   540\t       >>> naive == aware\n   541\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   542\t       ...\n   543\t       TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes\n   544\t\n   545\t   If you encounter this error, most likely your code is comparing these two\n   546\t   things:\n   547\t\n   548\t   - a datetime provided by Django -- for instance, a value read from a form or\n   549\t     a model field. Since you enabled time zone support, it's aware.\n   550\t   - a datetime generated by your code, which is naive (or you wouldn't be\n   551\t     reading this).\n   552\t\n   553\t   Generally, the correct solution is to change your code to use an aware\n   554\t   datetime instead.\n   555\t\n   556\t   If you're writing a pluggable application that's expected to work\n   557\t   independently of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ`, you may find\n   558\t   :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` useful. This function returns the current\n   559\t   date and time as a naive datetime when ``USE_TZ = False`` and as an aware\n   560\t   datetime when ``USE_TZ = True``. You can add or subtract\n   561\t   :class:`datetime.timedelta` as needed.\n   562\t\n   563\t#. **I see lots of** ``RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField received a naive\n   564\t   datetime`` ``(YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS)`` ``while time zone support is active``\n   565\t   **-- is that bad?**\n   566\t\n   567\t   When time zone support is enabled, the database layer expects to receive\n   568\t   only aware datetimes from your code. This warning occurs when it receives a\n   569\t   naive datetime. This indicates that you haven't finished porting your code\n   570\t   for time zone support. Please refer to the :ref:`migration guide\n   571\t   <time-zones-migration-guide>` for tips on this process.\n   572\t\n   573\t   In the meantime, for backwards compatibility, the datetime is considered to\n   574\t   be in the default time zone, which is generally what you expect.\n   575\t\n   576\t#. ``now.date()`` **is yesterday! (or tomorrow)**\n   577\t\n   578\t   If you've always used naive datetimes, you probably believe that you can\n   579\t   convert a datetime to a date by calling its :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date`\n   580\t   method. You also consider that a :class:`~datetime.date` is a lot like a\n   581\t   :class:`~datetime.datetime`, except that it's less accurate.\n   582\t\n   583\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   584\t\n   585\t       >>> import datetime\n   586\t       >>> import pytz\n   587\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   588\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   589\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   590\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   591\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   592\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   593\t       (True, False)\n   594\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   595\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   596\t       >>> paris\n   597\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   598\t       >>> new_york\n   599\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   600\t\n   601\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   602\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   603\t   fundamental.\n   604\t\n   605\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   606\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   607\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   608\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   609\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   610\t\n   611\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   612\t\n   613\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   614\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   615\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   616\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   617\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   618\t\n   619\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   620\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. Usually, this\n   621\t   will be the current timezone::\n   622\t\n   623\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   624\t       >>> timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(\"Asia/Singapore\"))\n   625\t       # For this example, we set the time zone to Singapore, but here's how\n   626\t       # you would obtain the current time zone in the general case.\n   627\t       >>> current_tz = timezone.get_current_timezone()\n   628\t       # Again, this is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   629\t       >>> local = current_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(current_tz))\n   630\t       >>> local\n   631\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 8, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Singapore' SGT+8:00:00 STD>)\n   632\t       >>> local.date()\n   633\t       datetime.date(2012, 3, 3)\n   634\t\n   635\t#. **I get an error** \"``Are time zone definitions for your database\n   636\t   installed?``\"\n   637\t\n   638\t   If you are using MySQL, see the :ref:`mysql-time-zone-definitions` section\n   639\t   of the MySQL notes for instructions on loading time zone definitions.\n   640\t\n   641\tUsage\n   642\t-----\n   643\t\n   644\t#. **I have a string** ``\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\"`` **and I know it's in the**\n   645\t   ``\"Europe/Helsinki\"`` **time zone. How do I turn that into an aware\n   646\t   datetime?**\n   647\t\n   648\t   This is exactly what pytz_ is for.\n   649\t\n   650\t       >>> from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime\n   651\t       >>> naive = parse_datetime(\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\")\n   652\t       >>> import pytz\n   653\t       >>> pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Helsinki\").localize(naive, is_dst=None)\n   654\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 21, 10, 28, 45, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Helsinki' EET+2:00:00 STD>)\n   655\t\n   656\t   Note that ``localize`` is a pytz extension to the :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   657\t   API. Also, you may want to catch ``pytz.InvalidTimeError``. The\n   658\t   documentation of pytz contains `more examples`_. You should review it\n   659\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   660\t\n   661\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   662\t\n   663\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   664\t\n   665\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   666\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   667\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   668\t\n   669\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   670\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   671\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. So, in most circumstances,\n   672\t   the datetime in UTC returned by :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` will be\n   673\t   sufficient.\n   674\t\n   675\t   For the sake of completeness, though, if you really want the local time\n   676\t   in the current time zone, here's how you can obtain it::\n   677\t\n   678\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   679\t       >>> timezone.localtime(timezone.now())\n   680\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 20, 10, 53, 873365, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   681\t\n   682\t   In this example, the current time zone is ``\"Europe/Paris\"``.\n   683\t\n   684\t#. **How can I see all available time zones?**\n   685\t\n   686\t   pytz_ provides helpers_, including a list of current time zones and a list\n   687\t   of all available time zones -- some of which are only of historical\n   688\t   interest. :mod:`zoneinfo` also provides similar functionality via\n   689\t   :func:`zoneinfo.available_timezones`.\n   690\t\n   691\t.. _pytz: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/\n   692\t.. _more examples: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#example-usage\n   693\t.. _these issues: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#problems-with-localtime\n   694\t.. _helpers: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#helpers\n   695\t.. _tz database: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database\n","message":"695 lines read from file starting from line 1. The main reason is daylight\n    19\tsaving time (DST). Many countries have a system of DST, where clocks are moved\n    20\tforward in spring and backward in autumn. If you're working in local time,\n    21\tyou're likely to encounter errors twice a year, when the transitions happen.\n    22\t(The pytz_ documentation discusses `these issues`_ in greater detail.) This\n    23\tprobably doesn't matter for your blog, but it's a problem if you over-bill or\n    24\tunder-bill your customers by one hour, twice a year, every year. The solution\n    25\tto this problem is to use UTC in the code and use local time only when\n    26\tinteracting with end users.\n    27\t\n    28\tTime zone support is disabled by default. To enable it, set :setting:`USE_TZ =\n    29\tTrue <USE_TZ>` in your settings file.\n    30\t\n    31\t.. note::\n    32\t\n    33\t    In Django 5.0, time zone support will be enabled by default.\n    34\t\n    35\tBy default, time zone support uses pytz_, which is installed when you install\n    36\tDjango; Django also supports the use of other time zone implementations like\n    37\t:mod:`zoneinfo` by passing :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` objects directly to\n    38\tfunctions in :mod:`django.utils.timezone`.\n    39\t\n    40\t.. versionchanged:: 3.2\n    41\t\n    42\t    Support for non-``pytz`` timezone implementations was added.\n    43\t\n    44\t.. note::\n    45\t\n    46\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n    47\t    startproject <startproject>` includes :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>`\n    48\t    for convenience.\n    49\t\n    50\tIf you're wrestling with a particular problem, start with the :ref:`time zone\n","message":"50 lines read from file starting from line 1. Instead, Django provides :ref:`time zone selection\n   164\tfunctions <time-zone-selection-functions>`. Use them to build the time zone\n   165\tselection logic that makes sense for you.\n   166\t\n   167\tMost websites that care about time zones ask users in which time zone they live\n   168\tand store this information in the user's profile. For anonymous users, they use\n   169\tthe time zone of their primary audience or UTC. pytz_ provides helpers_, like a\n   170\tlist of time zones per country, that you can use to pre-select the most likely\n   171\tchoices.\n   172\t\n   173\tHere's an example that stores the current timezone in the session.
